q: Which of the following processes would lead to an increase in variation among individuals of a population?
options: propagation of the species by underground tubers
exchange of chromatid segments during meiosis
independent behaviour of chromosomes during mitotic divisions
skill: Describe sources of variation, and differentiate between continuous and discontinuous variation in a population
feedback: During fertilisation, 1 gamete from each parent combines to form a zygote. Because of recombination and independent assortment in meiosis, each gamete contains a different set of DNA. This produces a unique combination of genes in the resulting zygote.

subject: Biology
q: Tay Sachs disease, which typically causes death in young children by the age of four, is caused by an abnormal allele of a single gene. Which is the most likely reason for why the disease has persisted in the human population?
options: The victims pass on their genes to subsequent generations.
It is caused by a dominant allele that is powerful and can be passed on more successfully.
It is caused by a recessive allele that does not show its effect in carriers.
The disease can be transmitted to other individuals via means other than genetic inheritance.
skill: Describe sources of variation, and differentiate between continuous and discontinuous variation in a population
feedback: Afflicted individuals are highly unlikely to pass the allele for the disease to subsequent generations since they die before they reach reproductive maturity. This being a genetic disease, cannot be transmitted by other means. Therefore, the reason it has persisted is because its caused by a recessive allele that does show its effect in carriers.

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following statements does NOT explain why body weight in antelopes exhibits continuous variation?
options: Within a population of antelopes, a broad range of body weights exists between low and high extremes.
Body weight in antelopes is easily affected by the availability of food in their habitat.
Only a few genes in antelopes determine body weight.
The genes controlling body weight in antelopes have an additive effect.
skill: Describe sources of variation, and differentiate between continuous and discontinuous variation in a population
feedback: Traits exhibiting continuous variation are controlled by more than one gene, therefore the statement only a few genes in antelopes determine a body weight is NOT correct.

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following correctly matches the given traits to the type of variation they exhibit?
options: intelligence - discontinuous, skin colour - continuous, ABO blood group - discontinuous
intelligence - continuous, skin colour - continuous, ABO blood group -discontinuous
intelligence - discontinuous, skin colour - discontinuous, ABO blood group -continuous
intelligence - continuous, skin colour - discontinuous, ABO blood group -continuous
skill: Describe sources of variation, and differentiate between continuous and discontinuous variation in a population
feedback:
Intelligence and skin colour are examples of continuous variation, whereas ABO blood group is an example of discontinuous variation.
Discontinuous variation is controlled by alleles of a single gene or a small number of genes. The environment has little effect on this type of variation. In continuous variation there is a complete range of measurements from one extreme to the other.

subject: Biology
q: Consider two different types of plants. One reproduces sexually while the other reproduces asexually. Which plant will be more likely to survive in a continually changing environment?
options: both plants are equally likely to survive
skill: Explain how variation can lead to evolution
feedback: The plants that reproduce sexually are more likely to survive in a continually changing environment. Sexual reproduction results in the generation of biological organisms with unique genetic makeup. On the contrary, asexual reproduction results in the generation of organisms with identical genetic makeup. In a continually changing environment, the organisms that reproduce sexually will have a better chance of survival. For example, if an infectious disease breaks out in plants then, plants that reproduce sexually are more likely to have plants with a unique genetic makeup that makes them resistant to the disease. On the other hand, if an infectious disease breaks out in plants that reproduce asexually then, the entire population would be wiped out since they all are genetically identical.

subject: Biology
q: _____ is/are the main source(s) of variation in a population of animals.
skill: Explain how variation can lead to evolution
feedback: Both mutations and synapsis are the main sources of variation in a population of animals. Mutations are directly responsible for producing alleles in a population. Synapsis is the genetic recombination of chromosomes during meiosis which results in the production of unique gametes. Thus, both mutations and synapsis are responsible for driving the variation in a population of animals, and even plants.

subject: Biology
q: Between brown bears and polar bears, which animal will be more likely to survive in the Arctic?
options: Brown bears will be more likely to survive as they are stronger.
Polar bears will be more likely to survive as their white fur acts as a camouflage.
Brown bears will be more likely to survive because they withstand colder temperatures better.
Polar bears will be more likely to survive because they can hibernate.
skill: Explain how variation can lead to evolution
feedback: Polar bears are more likely to survive in the Arctic compared to brown bears. The white fur of polar bears camouflages them from their prey. On the other hand, brown bears can be easily spotted in the white landscape of the Arctic which is why it must be difficult for them to hunt.

subject: Biology
q: Variations in the traits of organisms occurs due to _____.
skill: Explain how variation can lead to evolution
feedback: Variations in the traits of organisms occurs due to alleles. Alleles are different forms of the same gene. As a result, they produce different phenotypes. For example, the gene for eye colour determines the colour of your eyes. Different alleles result in different eye colour.

subject: Biology
q: Charles Darwin proposed that organisms produce many more offspring than can possibly survive on the limited amount of resources available to them. According to Darwin, the offspring that are most likely to survive are those that _____.
options: are born first and grow fastest
are the largest and most aggressive
are best adapted to the environment
skill: Explain how variation can lead to evolution
feedback:
The mechanism that Darwin proposed for evolution is natural selection. Because resources are limited in nature, organisms with heritable traits that favour survival and reproduction will tend to leave more offspring than their peers, causing the traits to increase in frequency over generations.

Natural selection causes populations to become adapted, or increasingly well-suited, to their environments over time. Natural selection depends on the environment and requires existing heritable variation in a group.

subject: Biology
q: Artificial selection involves the inbreeding of animals with _____.
skill: Describe artificial selection as a means of improvement of yield in economically important plants and animals
feedback: Artificial selection involves the inbreeding of animals with desirable characteristics. For example, to increase the milk yield of cows, only those cows are mated further that have a high yield of milk production. Cows with a low milk yield are not bred any further so that their genes are not passed down to the next generation.

subject: Biology
q: Wild bananas contain seeds as opposed to bananas we eat. The evolution of wild bananas into present day bananas must have occurred through _____.
skill: Describe artificial selection as a means of improvement of yield in economically important plants and animals
feedback: The evolution of wild bananas into present day bananas occurred through artificial selection. Wild bananas contain seeds as opposed to modern bananas. Through careful selective mating of plants by humans, wild bananas evolved into seedless bananas.

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following parts of the Earth is NOT a part of the biosphere?
skill: Describe levels of ecological organisation
feedback:
Earth's core is NOT a part of the biosphere. All the ecosystems on Earth are collectively called biosphere. No ecosystem is found at the Earth's core which is why it is not considered a part of the biosphere.
Troposphere is the lowest region of Earth's atmosphere and stratosphere is the second layer as you up. Life is found at the ocean floor, troposphere, and stratosphere.

subject: Biology
q: A wildlife sanctuary is an area where wildlife is protected in their natural habitats. A wildlife sanctuary is an example of a/an _____.
skill: Describe levels of ecological organisation
feedback: A wildlife sanctuary is an example of an ecosystem. Ecosystem is the collection of the all biotic and abiotic components of an environment and their interaction with one another. A wildlife sanctuary consists of organisms belonging to different species along with multiple habitats.

subject: Biology
q:
Fill in the blank below for a typical food chain.

producer \[\rightarrow\] primary consumer \[\rightarrow\] secondary consumer \[\rightarrow\]_____
skill: Describe levels of ecological organisation
feedback: A food chain represents a series of events in which food and energy are transferred from one organism in an ecosystem to another. Food chains show how energy is passed from the sun to producers, from producers to primary consumers, and eventually to tertiary consumers.

subject: Biology
q: Organisms in the ecosystem that are responsible for the recycling of plant and animal wastes are
skill: Describe levels of ecological organisation
feedback: The group of organisms called decomposers forms the final link in the food chain. They break down dead animals and plants and return vital nutrients to the soil. Some decomposers, like fungi, can be seen without a microscope, but much of the decomposition process is carried out by microscopic bacteria.

subject: Biology
q: The study of the interrelationship between organisms and their environment is called _____
skill: Describe levels of ecological organisation
feedback: Ecology is the branch of biology which studies the interactions among organisms and their environment. Objects of study include interactions of organisms with each other and with the abiotic components of their environment.

subject: Biology
q:
Consider the food chain below.

grass \[\rightarrow\] rabbit \[\rightarrow\] fox \[\rightarrow\] bear \[\rightarrow\] mushroom

How many types of decomposers are present?
skill: Describe levels of ecological organisation
feedback:
In the above food chain, only mushrooms are decomposers.

Organic matter is recycled in an ecosystem by decomposers. Decomposers are organisms such as bacteria and fungi that break down the organic matter in the dead bodies of plants and animals. As the decomposers feed from the dead animals, they break down the organic compounds into simple nutrients.

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following is not an artificial ecosystem?
topic: The ecosystem: levels of ecological organisation
skill: Differentiate between biotic and abiotic components of the ecosystem
feedback: Artificial ecosystems are man-made systems, like a zoo, a park and a garden. Natural ecosystems exist naturally. For example, an ocean, jungle or desert are examples of natural ecosystems.

subject: Biology
q: Two abiotic factors that affect plants within an ecosystem are _____.
skill: Define ecosystem, and describe its major components - Biology
feedback: The living things in an ecosystem are called the biotic components, while the non-living things in an ecosystem are called the abiotic components. Plants, animals, and bacteria are examples of biotic components, while temperature, soil, and water are examples of abiotic components. Hence, the abiotic components affecting an ecosystem are soil and water.

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following is not an artificial ecosystem?
skill: Define ecosystem, and describe its major components - Biology
feedback: Artificial ecosystems are man-made systems, like a zoo, a park and a garden. Natural ecosystems exist naturally. For example, an ocean, jungle or desert are examples of natural ecosystems.

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following correctly represents an abiotic factor as the cause of a disease?
options: diarrhoea due to bacteria present in drinking water
arsenicosis due to heavy metal arsenic remains present in drinking water
food poisoning due to bacteria present in food and water
ringworm due to a fungal growth on the skin
skill: Define ecosystem, and describe its major components - Biology
feedback: Abiotic factors are all the non-living things within an ecosystem. As arsenic metal is the only non-living thing in the options, it is the correct answer.

subject: Biology
q:
Imagine that an ecosystem possesses the following biotic components.
 
If, for some reason, the deer is removed from this ecosystem, what will be the impact on the other members of the ecosystem?
options: The number of lions will increase, and the growth of grass will decrease.
The number of lions will decrease, and the growth of grass will increase.
The number of lions will decrease, and the growth of grass will decrease as well.
The number of lions will increase, and the growth of grass will increase as well.
skill: Define ecosystem, and describe its major components - Biology
feedback: If we remove deer from this ecosystem, it will affect both the lions' population and the grass's growth. In the absence of the deer population, the grass will grow freely. As lions feed on deer, they will find it difficult to survive, and their number will decrease.

subject: Biology
q: A researcher identifies two different types of bacteria. She categorizes one as a producer while the other as a decomposer. What must be the difference between the two bacteria?
options: presence or absence of cell wall
topic: The flow of materials and energy in the ecosystem
skill: Describe the trophic levels of an ecosystem, and their energy relations
feedback:
Producers are organisms that have the capacity to synthesise their own food via photosynthesis. Recall the importance of chlorophyll to carry out photosynthesis. It is a pigment that absorbs sunlight and consequently, helps in synthesising complex organic compounds from inorganic materials. Therefore, the first bacterium must have chlorophyll since it is a producer, whereas the second bacterium must be a decomposer.
All the other options are irrelevant to an organism's trophic level. An organism's trophic level is determined by the source of its food.

subject: Biology
q: A scientist discovers a new species of beetles that feed on aphids. Which trophic level should the beetle be assigned to?
topic: The flow of materials and energy in the ecosystem
skill: Describe the trophic levels of an ecosystem, and their energy relations
feedback: Aphids are small insects that feed on plants. They are primary consumers. Since the new species of beetles feeds on primary consumers, they must be categorised as secondary consumers.

subject: Biology
q: Which trophic level has organisms that feed on organisms belonging to EVERY other trophic level?
topic: The flow of materials and energy in the ecosystem
skill: Describe the trophic levels of an ecosystem, and their energy relations
feedback:
Decomposers have the capacity to break down complex organic compounds. Since all organisms are made up of organic compounds, decomposers can feed on organisms belonging to every other trophic level.
Producers have their energy needs met via the sun.
Secondary consumers feed only on producers.
Tertiary consumers can feed on both primary consumers and producers.

subject: Biology
q: Which trophic level has organisms with the highest intake of energy?
topic: The flow of materials and energy in the ecosystem
skill: Describe the trophic levels of an ecosystem, and their energy relations
feedback: The flow of energy in trophic levels is unidirectional. This means it flows in only one direction from producers to consumers to decomposers. Since the sun is the source of all energy in most ecosystems, producers have the highest intake of energy. All the organisms in the next trophic levels must have a lower intake of energy compared to the producers.

subject: Biology
q: How can a food chain be affected by changes in the Earth's climate?
options: Climate change doesn't affect any food chain or food web.
Tigers and lions would stop hunting in winters and summers.
Climate change can cause extinction of a species in an ecosystem.
All animals would start starving due to climate change.
skill: Construct and describe food chains and webs, and flow of energy
feedback:
Climate changes can affect a food chain in many ways.
It can cause some species in an ecosystem to migrate if they are not adapted to the new climate. It can also cause the extinction of some species, which can disturb the whole food web.

subject: Biology
q: When we eat onions, our trophic level is that of a _____.
skill: Construct and describe food chains and webs, and flow of energy
feedback: Primary consumers eat plants directly. The secondary consumer is the organism that eats the primary consumer. So, when we eat onions, our trophic level is that of a primary consumer.

subject: Biology
q: In ecosystems, the flow of _____ is one way, while _____ is/are constantly recycled.
skill: Construct and describe food chains and webs, and flow of energy
feedback: An ecosystem survives by a combination of one-way energy flow and minerals that are recycled. Minerals and energy tend to flow in the same direction for most of an ecosystem. The big difference is that the minerals are ultimately recycled in the ecosystem while the energy is ultimately lost from the ecosystem to the universe at large.

subject: Biology
q:
Consider the following food chain:

tree \[\rightarrow\] caterpillar \[\rightarrow\] robin \[\rightarrow\] hawk \[\rightarrow\] coyote

Identify the secondary consumer.
skill: Construct and describe food chains and webs, and flow of energy
feedback:
Primary consumers eat plants exclusively and are all herbivores. The secondary consumer is the organism that eats the primary consumer. This animal is normally larger than the primary consumer but smaller than the tertiary consumer.

In above food chain, the tree is a producer, the caterpillar is a primary consumer, the robin is a secondary consumer, and the hawk and coyote are tertiary consumers.

subject: Biology
q: The total amount of living or organic matter in an ecosystem at any time is called _____.
skill: Construct and describe food chains and webs, and flow of energy
feedback: The total amount of dry weight of living or organic matter in an ecosystem at any time is called biomass.

subject: Biology
q: In the food chains shown, which organism would have the least amount of energy available?
topic: The flow of materials and energy in the ecosystem
skill: Interpret pyramids of numbers, energy and biomass
feedback: The lion would have the least amount of energy available. The flow of energy in any ecosystem is always unidirectional. With every following trophic level, the amount of energy available for the organisms, decreases. Therefore, the least amount of energy is available at the highest trophic level. The first food chain has three trophic levels and the second food chain has four trophic levels.

subject: Biology
q: Which trophic level has the highest energy consumption?
topic: The flow of materials and energy in the ecosystem
skill: Interpret pyramids of numbers, energy and biomass
feedback: The sun is our source of energy and is used by plants to produce their food. Therefore, the highest energy consumption is of producers. With every following trophic level, the amount of energy available for organisms decreases.

subject: Biology
q: Consider the food chain shown below. Which organism would be at bottom of the pyramid of numbers?
topic: The flow of materials and energy in the ecosystem
skill: Interpret pyramids of numbers, energy and biomass
feedback: In a pyramid of numbers, the producer, in this case corn, is always at the bottom. Each following trophic level is stacked on top of the previous trophic level to form a pyramid.

subject: Biology
q: A researcher is conducting an ecological study on a small island. She observes that the concentration of carbon dioxide in the air has steadily increased over the last five years. She claims that the amount of carbon on Earth has increased. Do you agree with her claim?
options: Yes, carbon dioxide has increased in the air, therefore there is more carbon on earth.
No, the amount of carbon has decreased as fewer fossil fuels are left.
No, the amount of carbon on Earth is fixed, it only changes from one form to another.
Yes, the amount of carbon has increased because of the burning of fossil fuels.
skill: Describe the carbon cycle
feedback: Although carbon can exist in various forms, the amount of carbon on Earth is constant. Carbon can be present in living things, fossil fuels, or in the air as carbon dioxide. It can change from one form to another, however, the total amount of carbon on Earth stays the same. Only high concentration of carbon dioxide (since it's a greenhouse gas) contributes towards global warming. Carbon, in any other form, doesn't contribute towards global warming.

subject: Biology
q: At what step of the given carbon cycle have there been changes that resulted in global warming?
skill: Describe the carbon cycle
feedback: For 200 years, human beings have been using fossil fuels to meet their energy requirements. However, as a result, more carbon dioxide has been released into environment. The combustion of fossil fuels has led to higher concentrations of carbon dioxide in the environment. Since carbon dioxide is a greenhouse gas, it has played a major role in increasing the temperature of the surface of the Earth.

subject: Biology
q: Which trophic level has organisms that are responsible for converting carbon dioxide into complex organic compounds?
skill: Describe the carbon cycle
feedback: Producers take carbon dioxide from the environment and convert it into complex organic compounds via photosynthesis. Only producers have the ability to do this. Organisms in every other trophic level rely on plants for carbon dioxide fixation.

subject: Biology
q: Which trophic level has organisms that are responsible for releasing carbon dioxide back into the environment?
skill: Describe the carbon cycle
feedback: All living beings, whether they are producers, consumers, or decomposers release carbon dioxide back into the environment. Organisms in every trophic level respire, therefore they exhale carbon dioxide into the environment. Decomposers release carbon dioxide by breaking down organic compounds of dead plants and animals.

subject: Biology
q: _____ is the conversion of nitrogen gas into nitrates.
skill: Describe the nitrogen cycle
feedback: The first step of the nitrogen cycle is nitrogen fixation. It refers to the conversion of nitrogen gas into nitrates and can occur via three possible ways, namely atmospheric, biological, or industrial.

subject: Biology
q: Plants form _____ as a result of assimilation.
skill: Describe the nitrogen cycle
feedback: Assimilation refers to the utilisation of nitrates by organisms. The nitrates absorbed by plants are used for making proteins. Proteins play an important role in the growth and survival of plants and animals. Nitrogen is an important component of protein.

subject: Biology
q: Which form of nitrogen is taken by the producers of the ecosystem?
skill: Describe the nitrogen cycle
feedback: Our atmosphere is a reservoir of free gaseous nitrogen. Producers of the ecosystem cannot pick up the gaseous nitrogen directly from atmosphere. It has to be converted into nitrates to be utilised by producers.

subject: Biology
q: _____ is the process of converting waste materials into new materials and objects.
skill: Describe possible actions to control pollution, including the principle of the '3 Rs' (reduce, reuse and recycle)
feedback: Recycling is the process of converting waste materials into new materials and objects. Recycling can prevent the waste of potentially useful materials and reduce the consumption of new raw materials, thereby reducing: energy usage, air pollution (from incineration), and water pollution (from landfilling).

subject: Biology
q: Recycling can help lower _____.
options: the rate of extinction of animals
skill: Describe possible actions to control pollution, including the principle of the '3 Rs' (reduce, reuse and recycle)
feedback: By recycling we can make new things from used items and we can also reduce waste disposal and pollution. Therefore, it is an alternative to waste disposal, which saves material and helps lower greenhouse gas emissions.

subject: Biology
q: When solid waste is _____, it produces harmful emissions.
skill: Describe possible actions to control pollution, including the principle of the '3 Rs' (reduce, reuse and recycle)
feedback: When the solid waste is burnt, it produces harmful emissions into the air.

subject: Biology
q: Solid waste should be managed properly by landfilling, _____ or recycling.
skill: Describe possible actions to control pollution, including the principle of the '3 Rs' (reduce, reuse and recycle)
feedback: Solid waste pollutes the Earth's environment when dumped in open spaces or burnt. Hence, we should not dump it in open places or burn it. Solid waste should be managed properly by landfill, incineration, or recycling.

subject: Biology
q: _____ is the method of burying solid waste.
skill: Describe possible actions to control pollution, including the principle of the '3 Rs' (reduce, reuse and recycle)
feedback: Landfill is a method of disposing trash and garbage by burying it between layers of earth.

subject: Biology
q: Burning waste material at a high temperature to produce gas or oil is called _____.
skill: Describe possible actions to control pollution, including the principle of the '3 Rs' (reduce, reuse and recycle)
feedback: Incineration is a method by which waste is burned at extremely high temperatures to produce gas or oil.

subject: Biology
q: Air, water, and soil are _____.
skill: Describe possible actions to control pollution, including the principle of the '3 Rs' (reduce, reuse and recycle)
feedback: The earth is the only planet where life is possible. Our survival depends on natural resources like air, water, and soil.

subject: Biology
q: We should _____ our natural resources for future use.
skill: Describe possible actions to control pollution, including the principle of the '3 Rs' (reduce, reuse and recycle)
feedback:
Conservation is the practice of caring for natural resources so that all living things can benefit from them now and in the future.
We should conserve, not only because it protects native plants, birds and animals, but also because it protects us.

subject: Biology
q: If we protect our environment by conservation and managing solid waste properly then we can prevent _____.
skill: Describe possible actions to control pollution, including the principle of the '3 Rs' (reduce, reuse and recycle)
feedback: If we protect our environment by conservation and managing solid waste properly then we can prevent the extinction of species.

subject: Biology
q: What important activity takes place in order to create awareness about the environment across the world?
options: people sing songs about the planet
movies are made about the food
skill: Describe possible actions to control pollution, including the principle of the '3 Rs' (reduce, reuse and recycle)
feedback: World Environment Day is celebrated on the 5th of June every year, with the aim of creating awareness and encouraging action to protect the environment.

subject: Biology
q: The image below is a symbol of _____.
skill: Describe possible actions to control pollution, including the principle of the '3 Rs' (reduce, reuse and recycle)
feedback: This symbol represents recycling. Recycling is the process of converting waste materials into new materials and objects.

subject: Biology
q: Apart from plastic, we can also recycle _____.
skill: Describe possible actions to control pollution, including the principle of the '3 Rs' (reduce, reuse and recycle)
feedback: Apart from plastic we can recycle things that we use every day like paper, soda cans, cartons and convert them into new products.

subject: Biology
q: When travelling short distances, the best way to conserve natural resources is _____.
skill: Describe possible actions to control pollution, including the principle of the '3 Rs' (reduce, reuse and recycle)
feedback: The best way to conserve natural resources is to walk or cycle when travelling short distances because they don't require any fuel. Using public transport, a motorbike, or carpooling is better than taking your own car, however these forms of travelling still require some fuel. Vehicles that require fuel release harmful emissions into the air, which contributes to environmental damages and causes health problems.

subject: Biology
q: The energy we use to heat our homes, drive our cars and run our computers comes from _____.
skill: Describe possible actions to control pollution, including the principle of the '3 Rs' (reduce, reuse and recycle)
feedback: The energy we use to heat our homes, drive our cars and run our computers comes from natural resources like oil, gas and water.

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following will help you save water?
options: shut the tap while brushing your teeth
skill: Describe possible actions to control pollution, including the principle of the '3 Rs' (reduce, reuse and recycle)
feedback: Water is the most essential natural resource. Without water life would not be possible on the earth. Therefore, it is our duty to save as much water as possible. One easy way of doing this is to close the tap while we brush our teeth.

subject: Biology
q: There are many types of air pollutants such as gases, soot, and _____.
skill: Discuss the impact of humans on the environment
feedback: There are many types of air pollutants such as gases, soot, and dust particles.

subject: Biology
q: The harmful gases released into the air include _____.
skill: Discuss the impact of humans on the environment
feedback: The harmful gases released into the air include sulphur dioxide, carbon monoxide, oxides of nitrogen and chlorofluorocarbons.

subject: Biology
q: The main sources of sulphur dioxide are _____.
skill: Discuss the impact of humans on the environment
feedback: The main sources of sulphur dioxide are electricity generation, burning of coal; oil and natural gas, industrial processes, and transportation (emissions from motor vehicles).

subject: Biology
q: Sulphur dioxide is a _____ gas with a very irritating smell.
skill: Discuss the impact of humans on the environment
feedback: Sulphur dioxide is a colourless gas and has a very irritating smell. Inhaling sulphur dioxide causes breathing difficulties, particularly for people with asthma and heart disease. Also, it is one of the causes of bronchitis and lung disease.

subject: Biology
q: The main sources of carbon monoxide are _____.
skill: Discuss the impact of humans on the environment
feedback:
Just like sulphur dioxide, carbon monoxide is also produced by emissions from motor vehicles. The chimneys of industries also emit this gas.
It's a colourless, tasteless and odourless gas.

subject: Biology
q: The harmful effects of carbon monoxide include _____ and _____.
skill: Discuss the impact of humans on the environment
feedback: Carbon monoxide causes symptoms of heart disease, such as chest pain. It may also result in vision problems and reduce physical and mental capabilities in healthy people.

subject: Biology
q: The short-term effects of pollution include illnesses like _____ or bronchitis.
skill: Discuss the impact of humans on the environment
feedback: The short-term effects of pollution, which are temporary, include illnesses such as pneumonia or bronchitis. They also include discomforts such as irritation to the nose, throat, eyes, or skin.

subject: Biology
q: What would happen if starfish were to be removed from a certain region?
options: There would be a decrease in the mussel population.
There would be an increase in the mussel population.
There would be an increase in the number of seaweeds.
There would be a decrease in the number of seaweeds.
skill: Discuss conservation for the sustainability of nature
feedback: Starfish eats mussels. if starfish are removed from a region in the ocean, mussels rapidly increase in number. A large number of mussels prey on small animals and become dangerous for their existence.

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following agencies published the red data book/ red list?
skill: Discuss conservation for the sustainability of nature
feedback: The International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN) is concerned with finding sustainable ways utilise natural resources and to conserve nature.

subject: Biology
q: When the last member of a particular species dies, the species is said to be ______.
skill: Discuss conservation for the sustainability of nature
feedback: A species that no longer exists is termed as extinct. Species that are near extinction are labelled as .

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following is most responsible for increased carbon dioxide in the atmosphere?
skill: Discuss conservation for the sustainability of nature
feedback: Fossil fuels contain large amounts of carbon, and when burned for energy, they release large amounts of carbon dioxide as a by-product. An increase in photosynthetic activity in plants would result in a decrease of atmospheric carbon dioxide, as that is a reactant in the process of photosynthesis. Ocean acidification and the melting of polar ice caps occurs as a result of the increase in atmospheric carbon dioxide, but they are not the cause of it.

subject: Biology
q:
Which of the following statements explain how an invasive species can threaten biodiversity in a geographic region?
I.   It can cause damage to the environment.
II.  It can harm the human economy.
III. It may compete with native species for food and habitat.
skill: Discuss conservation for the sustainability of nature
feedback: Invasive species are introduced species that become well-established in their new environment, spread easily and cause ecological and economic harm. They frequently outcompete native species and lead to the decline or even extinction of natives such as different types of plants, animals, fungi, and microorganisms.

subject: Biology
q:
Which of the following points regarding extinction is true?
I.   The biodiversity of the earth has remained constant because speciation and extinction rates are always equal.
II.  Extinctions caused by human activity are higher than the natural extinction rate.
III. Extinction occurs only as a result of natural disasters, such as asteroids or volcanic activity.
skill: Discuss conservation for the sustainability of nature
feedback:
Biodiversity is affected by the balance between the speciation and extinction rates, and these are not always equal. When the extinction rate exceeds the speciation rate, biodiversity experiences a decline. Historically, the natural extinction rate is between 1-5 extinctions per year. The impact of human activities has caused this rate to jump to a significantly higher rate, offsetting the balance of biodiversity.
Although natural disasters can cause extinction events to occur, human activities have had a massive impact on the extinction rates on the planet.

subject: Biology
q: Which human activity would most likely deplete non-renewable resources?
options: using natural predators to eliminate pests
skill: Discuss conservation for the sustainability of nature
feedback: If human population continues to grow at an exponential rate, non-renewable resources (ex: fossil fuels) are bound to run out. However, regulating industrial pollution, developing wildlife sanctuaries and using natural predators to eliminate pests would have a positive impact on the environment.

subject: Biology
q: When the last member of a particular species dies, the species is said to be
skill: Discuss conservation for the sustainability of nature
feedback: Extinction is the termination of an organism or of a group of organisms (taxon), normally a species. The moment of extinction is generally considered to be the death of the last individual of the species, although the capacity to breed and recover may have been lost before this point. Because a species' potential range may be very large, determining this moment is difficult, and is usually done retrospectively.

subject: Biology
q: Nitrogen oxides may naturally occur in our atmosphere from _____.
skill: Discuss the impact of humans on the environment
feedback: Nitrogen oxides are emitted into the atmosphere from motor vehicles and industrial processes. But they naturally occur in our atmosphere too, from volcanoes, oceans and lightning strikes.

subject: Biology
q: The oxides of nitrogen combine with fog to form _____.
skill: Discuss the impact of humans on the environment
feedback: The oxides of nitrogen combine with fog to form smog. The smog causes asthma, coughing and wheezing.

subject: Biology
q: Chlorofluorocarbons can cause serious health issues such as _____ and damages eyesight.
skill: Discuss the impact of humans on the environment
feedback: Chlorofluorocarbons come from sprays; foam and plastics for making disposable fast food containers; refrigeration into the air. They can cause serious health issues such as skin cancer and eye damage.

subject: Biology
q: The short-term effects of pollution include illnesses like _____ or bronchitis.
skill: Discuss the impact of humans on the environment
feedback: The short-term effects of pollution, which are temporary, include illnesses such as pneumonia or bronchitis. They also include discomforts such as irritation to the nose, throat, eyes, or skin.

subject: Biology
q: What is the main reason of the melting of ice sheets?
options: an increase in the amount of oxygen
a decrease in the amount of carbon dioxide
skill: Explain the greenhouse effect and global warming - Biology
feedback: Global warming is affecting various parts of the world. It is accelerating the melting of ice sheets, permafrost, and glaciers, thus increasing the average sea levels to a great extent.

subject: Biology
q: _____ will affect agriculture and food production around the world.
skill: Explain the greenhouse effect and global warming - Biology
feedback: Climate change will affect agriculture and food production around the world. For example, factors such as projected increases in temperatures, changes in precipitation patterns, extreme weather conditions, and a decrease in the availability of water may all result in reduced agricultural productivity.

subject: Biology
q: A greenhouse is made of _____.
skill: Explain the greenhouse effect and global warming - Biology
feedback: A greenhouse is a house made of glass. It has glass walls and a glass roof. Sunlight enters and refracts, thus getting trapped inside. Along with sunlight, heat also enters the greenhouse, thus making it warm inside. People grow tomatoes, flowers, and other plants inside the greenhouse under controlled climatic conditions. A greenhouse stays warm even during winter.

subject: Biology
q: At night, most of the sun's energy
options: helps in the growth of plants.
skill: Explain the greenhouse effect and global warming - Biology
feedback: Earth's atmosphere does the same thing as the greenhouse. Gases in the atmosphere such as carbon dioxide do what the roof of a greenhouse does. During the day, the Sun shines through the atmosphere. Earth's surface warms up due to the sunlight. At night, Earth's surface cools down and releases the heat into the air. However, some of the heat is trapped by the greenhouse gases in the atmosphere. This is what keeps our Earth warm.

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following contributes to both global warming and acid rain?
skill: Describe the causes of acid rain, and its harmful effects - Biology
feedback:
The smoke produced by thermal power stations contributes to both global warming and acid rain.
The coal that is burned to generate electricity emits CO2, SO2, and nitrogen oxides (NOx).
CO2 contributes to global warming by increasing the greenhouse effect, and SO2 and nitrogen oxides contribute to acid rain.

subject: Biology
q:
Which of the following measures can be taken to stop acid rain?

I.   riding bicycles or walking instead of using automobiles
II.  using wind turbines and solar panels to generate electricity
III. by planting more trees
skill: Describe the causes of acid rain, and its harmful effects - Biology
feedback:
Acid rain is mainly caused by the sulphur dioxide and nitrogen oxides that is released when fuel is burned.
Thermal power stations and industries contribute the most towards causing acid rain.
We can reduce the amount of acid rain by generating electricity using wind and solar power stations and by using bicycles or walking where possible.

Planting more trees is not helpful in reducing acid rain, because plants can't absorb sulphur dioxide and nitrogen oxide. Rather, the growth of plants is also affected by acid rain.

subject: Biology
q: Abdullah collects a sample of acid rain and put litmus paper into it. What will happen to the litmus paper?
options: The blue litmus paper will red.
The red litmus paper turned blue.
The blue litmus paper remained blue.
The red litmus paper turned green.
skill: Describe the causes of acid rain, and its harmful effects - Biology
feedback:
Acid rain is any form of precipitation that is acidic.
Acids turn blue litmus paper to red.
We can test for the acidic nature of acid rain by putting blue litmus paper in it.

subject: Biology
q:
Complete this reaction for acid rain.

water + _____ → sulphurous acid
skill: Describe the causes of acid rain, and its harmful effects - Biology
feedback:
The reaction for production of sulphuric acid is

water + sulphur dioxide → sulphurous acid
Or
H2O + SO2 → H2SO3.

subject: Biology
q:
Which of the following can occur naturally?

I.   greenhouse effect
II.  acid rain
III. deforestation
skill: Describe the causes of acid rain, and its harmful effects - Biology
feedback:
The greenhouse effect, acid rain, and deforestation all may occur naturally.

The greenhouse effect occurs naturally due to the layer of gases present in our atmosphere. This layer of gases allows heat to enter the Earth and stops it from moving back out. Thus, the Earth's temperature is maintained at night in the absence of the Sun.
The natural causes of acid rain are volcanoes and decaying vegetation. Volcanoes release sulphur dioxide gas.
Deforestation can occur naturally due to drought or wildfire.

subject: Biology
q: Deforestation will most likely result in an increase in _____.
skill: Describe the effect of humans on wildlife habitats, and deforestation, and energy resources - Biology
feedback: Deforestation (the cutting of trees) stops trees from removing carbon dioxide from the atmosphere through photosynthesis. So, when we cut down trees, levels of carbon dioxide in the atmosphere will increase. And a higher level of carbon dioxide in the atmosphere contributes to global warming.

subject: Biology
q: One irreversible effect of both deforestation and water pollution on the environment is the _____.
options: decrease in atmospheric carbon dioxide levels
skill: Describe the effect of humans on wildlife habitats, and deforestation, and energy resources - Biology
feedback: Irreversible means that you cannot undo it. Once an organism is extinct, it cannot come back. When we cut down trees, we remove habitats and food sources for numerous species that rely on them for survival. Similarly, water pollution is harming animals that rely on water for their survival. Both these factors can result in the extinction of wildlife.

subject: Biology
q: The image shows a section of nitrogenous bases on one of the old strands - A. After DNA replication, what will be the base sequences of strands B and D?
options: Both will have the sequence ATTGGTC.
Both will have the sequence CGGTTCA.
skill: Define biotechnology, and discuss its importance
feedback:
During DNA replication, new bases are added following the rule of base pairing. The rule states that adenine (A) is always paired with thymine (T) and guanine (G) is always paired with (C) cytosine.
The new strand B and the old strand D are both complementary to strand A. Therefore, both B and D will have a base sequence of ATTGGTC complementary to TAACCAG.

subject: Biology
q: A strand that has bases ACGAT produces a strand with the complementary bases
skill: Define biotechnology, and discuss its importance
feedback: During DNA replication, new nucleotides join to form a new strand. The strand that has bases ACGAT produces a strand with the complementary bases TGCTA. This is in accordance with the base-pairing rule which states that A is always paired with T and G is always paired with C.

subject: Biology
q: Which bonds hold together the nitrogenous bases on a DNA strand?
skill: Define biotechnology, and discuss its importance
feedback: Strands of DNA are made of the sugar and phosphate groups of the nucleotides, while the middle parts are made of the nitrogenous bases - A, T, G, C. The nitrogenous bases on the two strands of DNA pair up and are held together by weak hydrogen bonds.

subject: Biology
q: The special enzyme that unwinds the double helix molecule of DNA is called
skill: Define biotechnology, and discuss its importance
feedback: Helicases are a class of enzymes vital to all living organisms. Their main function is to unzip an organism's DNA. Helicase unwinds the DNA and separates and unzips the double-stranded DNA.

subject: Biology
q: Yeast is used to produce bread. During this process, the bread rises up due to _____.
skill: Compare methods of fermentation by yeast and bacteria
feedback: While baking, the bread rises up due to the release of carbon dioxide by yeast. Yeast depends on alcoholic fermentation to produce energy necessary for its cellular activities. In alcoholic fermentation, glucose is broken down into ethanol and carbon dioxide. Ethanol is the intoxicating molecule present in alcoholic beverages.

subject: Biology
q: The following cell must rely _____ for energy production.
skill: Compare methods of fermentation by yeast and bacteria
feedback: The following cell must rely on alcoholic fermentation for energy production. Since this cell is dividing through the process of budding, we can conclude that it is a yeast cell. Yeast relies on alcoholic fermentation in order to generate energy necessary for cellular activities.

subject: Biology
q: In the formation of acrylic acid, _____ is/are used.
skill: Compare methods of fermentation by yeast and bacteria
feedback: The genus Bacillus includes a variety of industrially important species that are commonly used as hosts in the fermentation industry. The products that are made through fermentations with Bacillus include enzymes, antibiotics, and acrylic acid.

subject: Biology
q: In the formation of glycerol, _____ is/are used.
skill: Compare methods of fermentation by yeast and bacteria
feedback: Glycerol production by yeast fermentation has been known since the investigations of Pasteur in the nineteenth century. In Saccharomyces cerevisiae, glycerol is a by-product of the fermentation of sugar to ethanol in a redox-neutral process.

subject: Biology
q: In the formation of ethanol, _____ is/are used.
skill: Compare methods of fermentation by yeast and bacteria
feedback: Alcoholic fermentation is carried out by many types of yeast such as Saccharomyces cerevisiae. The product, acetaldehyde, is then reduced to ethanol.

subject: Biology
q: In the formation of formic acid, _____ is/are used.
skill: Compare methods of fermentation by yeast and bacteria
feedback: When formic acid is produced industrially, it is a product of fermentation using the Aspergillus microorganism. Formic acid is used in textile dyeing, leather treatment, electroplating, and rubber manufacture.

subject: Biology
q: Transgenic bacteria are used to produce insulin through fermenters. A bioengineer adds a gene for insulin and a gene for resistance against ciprofloxacin in the plasmid of Escherichia coli, which is the bacteria used to produce insulin. Which antibiotic can be used to sterilise the fermenter for this process?
skill: Relate the working of a fermenter with its structure and the advantages of using one
feedback: Ciprofloxacin can be used to sterilise the fermenter in this case. Since the transgenic bacteria also carries a gene for resistance against ciprofloxacin, this antibiotic will kill unwanted bacteria but not Escherichia coli. All the other antibiotics will also kill this bacterium since it lacks the antibiotic resistant genes for them.

subject: Biology
q: Why is a fermenter sterilised before fermentation can be carried out?
skill: Relate the working of a fermenter with its structure and the advantages of using one
feedback:
All the options. Fermenters are sterilised to prevent contamination by killing unwanted microorganisms. As a result, the yield of the desired product also increases since the useful bacteria will grow more because of more media availability.
If unwanted microorganisms are present in the fermenters then, they will compete with the beneficial bacteria for nutrients. As a result, the yield of the products of fermentation may decrease.

subject: Biology
q: How is a fermenter sterilised before fermentation can be carried out?
options: by adding bleach in the raw materials
by adding chlorine in the raw materials
skill: Relate the working of a fermenter with its structure and the advantages of using one
feedback: Fermenters are sterilised by passing steam through them. Bleach, chlorine, and broad-spectrum antibiotics are used to kill microorganisms, but they can't be used since they will also kill the bacteria and fungi that carry out fermentation.

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following must be regulated to ensure the optimum growth of microorganisms in a fermenter?
skill: Relate the working of a fermenter with its structure and the advantages of using one
feedback: All the options. The pH, temperature, and amount of nutrients must be regulated to ensure the optimum growth of microorganisms in a fermenter. Living organisms require optimum environmental conditions to thrive. Other environmental factors that must be regulated in a fermenter are oxygen levels and the amount of growth inhibitors in the media.

subject: Biology
q: For the production of single-celled proteins, the microorganisms are grown in _____.
skill: Relate the working of a fermenter with its structure and the advantages of using one
feedback: A fermenter is a device that provides optimum environment to microorganisms to grow into a biomass, so that they can interact with a substrate, forming the product.

subject: Biology
q: Single cell proteins have a high nitrogenous content. Which of the following diseases can occur as a result of single cell proteins?
topic: Single cell protein and its uses
skill: Describe single-cell protein and its importance
feedback:
High nitrogenous content may lead to excess uric acid which causes gout. It occurs when high levels of uric acid are present in blood.
Myocardial infarction is the scientific term for heart attack.
Diabetes is a disease that affects an individual's ability to regulate blood glucose levels.
Stroke occurs as a result of the death of brain cells. It can occur either due to lack of blood flow to the brain or bleeding.

subject: Biology
q: Single cell proteins contain _____.
options: high vitamin content and all essential amino acids
topic: Single cell protein and its uses
skill: Describe single-cell protein and its importance
feedback:
Single cell proteins contain high vitamin content and all essential amino acids. Therefore, they have a high nutritional value. There are twenty different amino acids. Some of these amino acids are produced by our bodies whereas, the remaining are supplemented by our diet. These remaining amino acids are called essential amino acids.
Lipids are not present in single cell proteins.

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following cannot be used to produce single cell proteins?
topic: Single cell protein and its uses
skill: Describe single-cell protein and its importance
feedback:
Single cell proteins can't be produced by viruses.
Unicellular organisms are used to produce single cell proteins. These unicellular organisms include bacteria, fungi, and algae.

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following can be combated through the production of single cell proteins?
topic: Single cell protein and its uses
skill: Describe single-cell protein and its importance
feedback:
Single cell proteins can be used to combat global warming, world hunger, and industrial pollution. Moreover, agricultural waste is also tackled through the production of single cell proteins.
The meat industry has one of the largest carbon footprints on the planet. Production of proteins through microbes will reduce the emission of greenhouse gases due to the meat industry. Furthermore, the increasingly high demand of food can't be met through conventional agricultural methods. Since the yield of single cell proteins is much higher than the yield of plants and meat, therefore, it is likely that the problem of world hunger can be addressed.
The unicellular organisms feed on industrial and agricultural waste to produce single cell proteins. Thus, they also help in reducing pollution.

subject: Biology
q: Single cell proteins are produced by microorganisms through _____.
topic: Single cell protein and its uses
skill: Describe single-cell protein and its importance
feedback: Single cell proteins are produced by microorganisms through fermentation. It is a chemical process that breaks down chemical compounds to produce energy in the absence of cellular respiration.

subject: Biology
q:
Bacteria are used to produce human insulin by genetic engineering. The process involves different steps.

W: The genetically modified plasmid is put back into the bacterium.
X: The gene that codes for insulin from human DNA is isolated and inserted into the gap in the plasmid.
Y: A plasmid is isolated from the bacterium and a small section is cut out.
Z: The bacterium is cloned to produce human insulin.

Which line of the table shows these steps in the correct order?
Options step 1 step 2step 3step 4
A \[Z\] \[W\] \[X\]\[Y\]
B \[W\] \[X\]\[Y\]\[Z\]
C \[Y\]\[X\]\[W\]\[Z\]
D \[X\] \[Y\] \[Z\]\[W\]
skill: Describe how a gene is transplanted
feedback:
The third line of the table shows the steps in correct order.
First, a plasmid is isolated from the bacterium and a small section is cut out of it with the help of an enzyme. The gene that codes for insulin from human DNA is isolated and inserted into the gap in the plasmid. The recombinant plasmid is put back into the bacterium. After that, the bacterium is cloned, so that each bacterial cell contains the human gene. These cells then produce the substances which are coded by the human gene.

subject: Biology
q: The figure below shows the process of
options: the production of insulin by bacteria through the process of genetic engineering.
the production of energy through the process of respiration in plants.
the production of food through the process of photosynthesis in plants.
transferring heat from a body at high temperature to a body at low temperature.
skill: Describe how a gene is transplanted
feedback:
The figure shows the different steps involved in the process of the production of insulin through genetically modified bacteria.
To introduce human genes into a bacterium, the insulin gene and plasmid are isolated. Then, with the help of restriction enzymes and DNA ligase, a cut is made in the plasmid, and the human gene is joined at the cut. The recombinant plasmid is then reintroduced into the bacterium which is cloned to produce the insulin.

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following statement is NOT true about genetic engineering?
options: Genetic engineering is commonly used in agriculture.
Genetic engineering is used to get rid of illnesses in young and unborn children.
Genetic engineering is a more efficient way of producing human insulin.
The role of enzymes is very limited in genetic engineering.
skill: Describe how a gene is transplanted
feedback: Enzymes play an important role in genetic engineering. Production of insulin through genetic engineering, for example, needs a few enzymes like molecular scissors and DNA ligase. Genetic engineering has application in agriculture, combating disease, and the production of useful products like insulin.

subject: Biology
q:
Read the saying below:

" Genetic engineering is a result of science advancement, so I don't think that in itself is bad. If used wisely, genetics can be beneficial, but they can be abused, too. "
Hideo Kojima

According to this saying, which of the following conclusions is the MOST reasonable?
options: Scientist always misuse the genetic engineering for their own benefits.
Genetic engineering has revolutionised agriculture and medicine.
Genetic engineering is generally beneficial for human kind, but it can be misused.
Genetic engineering is a complicated process.
skill: Describe how a gene is transplanted
feedback: The most reasonable conclusion is that genetic engineering is beneficial for human beings. It is being used in agriculture, medicine, and other fields to benefit human kind. But there are also some chances that it can be misused against humanity or environment. Scientists have found out that there are a few negative effects of some genetically produced chemicals on the development of animal embryos.

subject: Biology
q: How can genetically modified bacteria clean up areas affected by an oil spill?
options: The bacteria increase the resistance of affected plants.
The bacteria produce enzymes that break down the oil.
The bacteria form a protective layer around the oil, so it doesn't spread.
The bacteria seal the source of the oil spill to stop further leakage.
skill: Describe the major achievements of genetic engineering with reference to improvement in agricultural crops
feedback: Oil spills cause widespread environmental damage. They kill and harm the plants and animals in the affected area and are hard to clean up. Their damage can be limited by using genetically modified bacteria that produce enzymes to break down the oil. Once its broken down, the oil is no longer harmful to plants and animals.

subject: Biology
q: The genetic modification of canola can decrease its bitterness. This is an example of genetic modification that
options: makes the crop resistant to disease.
kills any weeds that steal the crops' nutrients.
skill: Describe the major achievements of genetic engineering with reference to improvement in agricultural crops
feedback: Canola is a type of oilseed that is commonly used as a vegetable oil. Some varieties have a bitter taste that people do not like. Genetic modification can be used to decrease the bitterness of the oil, therefore improving its flavour.

subject: Biology
q: What is the main benefit of genetically modifying rice so it contains more vitamin A?
options: The extra vitamin A improves the flavour of rice.
The rice has an attractive golden colour and more people buy it.
Vitamin A is good for vision and improves eyesight.
Vitamin A is good for bones as it helps with the absorption of calcium.
skill: Describe the major achievements of genetic engineering with reference to improvement in agricultural crops
feedback:
Genetically modified rice that contains extra vitamin A is known as golden rice. Vitamin A is good for vision and improves eyesight. Golden rice is especially beneficial in countries where the population has a deficiency of vitamin A.

While the rice does have a golden colour, this is not the reason for genetically modifying it.

subject: Biology
q: How are soil pollutants like mercury and trinitrotoluene (TNT) affected by genetically modified plants?
options: The plants absorb the pollutants and remove them from the soil.
The plants change the pollutants into their gaseous state.
The plants make the animals in the area resistant to these pollutants.
The plants produce enzymes that break down the pollutants.
skill: Describe the major achievements of genetic engineering with reference to improvement in agricultural crops
feedback: Enzymes produced by certain types of bacteria can break down soil pollutants into substances that are not harmful. Through genetic modification, the genes that make these enzymes can be inserted into plants. The plants then produce enzymes to break down the pollutants.

subject: Biology
q: Suppose a species of rice has one of the following properties. Which property would genetic engineers be MOST interested in?
options: It can remain flooded in water for several weeks without dying.
It attracts all sorts of insects that quickly destroy the crop.
It can only grow properly within a narrow range of temperature.
It quickly absorbs and retains any pesticide or herbicide sprayed on it.
skill: Describe the major achievements of genetic engineering with reference to improvement in agricultural crops
feedback: Farmers lose a large percentage of crops every year because of disease, extreme weather, and lack of proper care. While rice crops normally need to be kept in water to grow properly, most varieties will die if they remain flooded for too long. A species that can remain flooded for several weeks without dying is valuable to genetic engineers. If they succeed in isolating the gene that gives rice this property, other varieties of rice can benefit as well.

subject: Biology
q: Genetic engineering is
options: the creation of buildings by engineers that are good for the environment.
a change in the genes of organisms that produces random features.
a change by humans in the genes of organisms to produce desirable features.
the adaptation of animals to their environments through a change in their genes.
skill: Describe the major achievements of genetic engineering with reference to improvement in agricultural crops
feedback: Genetic engineering, also known as genetic modification, is a change by humans in the genes of organisms to produce desirable features. Genetic engineering is already being used in fields like medicine and agriculture and has the potential to play a large role in improving the quality of life of humans in the future.

subject: Biology
q: What substance is injected into the body to treat a person with diabetes?
skill: Describe the major achievements of genetic engineering in health and medicine
feedback: Diabetes affects the body's ability to produce or use a hormone called insulin. This hormone is produced by the pancreas and regulates the body's blood sugar level. Some diabetics have to receive regular insulin injections to live a healthy life.

subject: Biology
q: How did doctors treat patients with diabetes in the early 20<sup>th</sup> century?
options: They injected patients with insulin taken from animals.
They injected patients with insulin made by yeast.
They injected patients with insulin made by bacteria.
They vaccinated patients by inserting a weak virus into the body.
skill: Describe the major achievements of genetic engineering in health and medicine
feedback: Before the 20th century, there was no cure for diabetes. People who had the disease fell seriously ill and often died. In the 1920s, doctors started to inject patients with insulin taken from animals. This procedure still had risks as people suffered from allergic reactions or did not respond to the injections.

subject: Biology
q: How do doctors treat patients with diabetes in the 21<sup>st</sup> century?
options: They inject patients with insulin taken from animals.
They inject patients with insulin made by bacteria.
They tell patients to eat foods rich in vitamin D.
They vaccinate patients by inserting a weak virus into the body.
skill: Describe the major achievements of genetic engineering in health and medicine
feedback: In the 1980s, scientists discovered a way to create pure insulin on a large scale using genetically modified bacteria. The insulin is then injected into the body. This method is effective for treating diabetes and is still used nowadays.

subject: Biology
q:
The steps below show how insulin is produced by genetically modified bacteria. Place these steps in the correct order.

I. The genetically modified bacterium starts dividing rapidly and makes insulin.
II. The gene responsible for insulin production in human cells is isolated.
III. The gene responsible for insulin production is joined with DNA from a bacterium.
IV. The genetically modified DNA is introduced into another bacterium cell.
skill: Describe the major achievements of genetic engineering in health and medicine
feedback:
The technique to make insulin using genetically modified bacteria was discovered in the 1980s. The correct order is:

II. The gene responsible for insulin production in human cells is isolated.
III. The gene responsible for insulin production is joined with DNA from a bacterium.
IV. The genetically modified DNA is introduced into another bacterium cell
I. The genetically modified bacterium starts dividing rapidly and makes insulin.

subject: Biology
q:
The statements below show the first three steps of creating a vaccine for people with measles.

I. The measles virus is taken from a person who has the disease.
II. The virus is placed in the cells of young chicks.
III. The virus weakens over time because of a change in its environment.

What is the next step in this process?
options: The weakened virus is injected into the tissue of cattle.
The gene that weakens the virus is isolated by scientists.
The cells of the young chicks are injected into fully-grown hens.
The weakened virus is injected into the tissue of humans.
skill: Describe the major achievements of genetic engineering in health and medicine
feedback: Once the virus has weakened, it is injected into the tissue of humans. The weakened virus stimulates the production of antibodies to guard the body against the measles virus in the future.

subject: Biology
q: What is a vaccine?
options: a substance used to stimulate the production of antibodies
the medium in which the gene of one organism is inserted into another
a cell that can grow to become any type of cell in the body
a substance in the bloodstream that reduces the number of white blood cells
skill: Describe the major achievements of genetic engineering in health and medicine
feedback: A vaccine is a substance used to stimulate the production of antibodies. These antibodies are the body's disease-fighting cells and are produced by white blood cells.

subject: Biology
q: Which invention is an example of biotechnology, but NOT genetic modification?
options: an artificial knee made of a material that does not react with the body
a hormone produced in a lab to treat a deficiency of red blood cells
a vaccine created by injecting a virus into chick cells
skill: Describe the major achievements of genetic engineering in health and medicine
feedback: Knees are important joints as they are essential for walking and bear a lot of the body's weight. Knee replacements, especially in old age, are common procedures because the joint can become weakened over time. In this procedure, an artificial knee is used and is made of a material that does not react with the body. The material is normally a combination of metal and plastic. No genes are modified in these artificial knees, so this is NOT an example of genetic modification.

subject: Biology
q: Cells that can grow to become any type of cell in the body are known as
skill: Describe the major achievements of genetic engineering in health and medicine
feedback: Stem cells are cells that can grow to become any type of cell in the body. They are of special interest to scientists and are being researched to see if they can cure a large variety of diseases.

subject: Biology
q: Which statement about genetic modification in medicine is TRUE?
options: There is no need to further research genetic modification in medicine because the risks are greater than the benefits.
There is no need to research the risks of genetic modification in medicine because there are none.
Genetic modification in medicine should continue to be researched.
All the benefits of genetic modification in medicine have been discovered.
skill: Describe the major achievements of genetic engineering in health and medicine
feedback: Genetic modification has led to several beneficial discoveries in medicine. The area should continue to be researched as many benefits are yet to be discovered. Like any new area of medicine, the risks should always be researched as well.

subject: Biology
q: Measles are treated with _____ before the _____ enters the body.
skill: Describe the major achievements of genetic engineering in health and medicine
feedback: Measles are treated with a vaccine before the virus enters the body. This vaccine provides the body with immunity to guard it against any viruses in the future.

subject: Biology
q: When a measles vaccine is injected into the tissue, special white blood cells produce antibodies. What happens to these cells and antibodies after a few years?
options: The antibodies go away but the blood cells that produced them remain.
The antibodies remain in the bloodstream till the end of the person's life.
The antibodies remain in the bloodstream as long as the person doesn't get measles again.
Both the blood cells and the antibodies they produce go away.
skill: Describe the major achievements of genetic engineering in health and medicine
feedback: Special white blood cells known as memory B cells produce antibodies that fight the viruses. The antibodies go away after a few weeks but the blood cells that produced them remain permanently in the bloodstream.

subject: Biology
q: Which step in the treatment of diabetes is similar to the treatment of measles?
options: In both treatments, substances are injected into the body before the person contracts the disease.
In both treatments, genetically modified substances are injected into the body.
The body produces antibodies as a result of both treatments.
The body produces glucose as a result of both treatments.
skill: Describe the major achievements of genetic engineering in health and medicine
feedback: People with diabetes receive injections of insulin made by genetically modified bacteria. People are vaccinated against measles by receiving an injection containing a weakened version of the virus. Therefore, in both treatments, genetically modified substances are injected into the body.

subject: Biology
q: Why do scientists like to use bacteria in the creation of insulin?
options: They naturally produce insulin at rapid rates.
They can be safely injected into the body.
skill: Describe the major achievements of genetic engineering in health and medicine
feedback: One of the benefits of genetic modification is that it allows for the rapid, large-scale manufacture of medicines that were previously unavailable. The organisms that are used in this manufacture need to have characteristics suited for this process. Bacteria are widely used because they can reproduce very quickly as this ensures the medicine is also produced quickly.

subject: Biology
q:
Which of the following processes involves genetic modification?

I. the production of insulin in the pancreas
II. the production of insulin by bacteria
III. the production of antibodies by white blood cells
IV. the creation of vaccines
skill: Describe the major achievements of genetic engineering in health and medicine
feedback: The production of insulin by the pancreas and antibodies by blood cells are normal bodily processes that don't involve genetic modification. Vaccines are created through different methods. Some involve genetic modification and others do not. The only process that always involves genetic modification is the production of insulin by bacteria.

subject: Biology
q: _____ is a pain killer produced by the brain.
skill: Describe the major achievements of genetic engineering in health and medicine
feedback: The correct answer is Beta-endorphin.

subject: Biology
q: The powdered form of _____ is applied on wounds to _____ and ______.
options: sodium nitrate; make them bleed; prevent infection
sodium nitrate; stop bleeding; dry out
silver nitrate; numb pain; inhibit infection
silver nitrate; stop bleeding; prevent infection
skill: Define pharmacology and drugs, and identify the various sources of drugs
feedback: The powdered form of silver nitrate is applied on wounds to stop bleeding and prevent infection. The powdered form of silver nitrate is a natural compound that is used as an anti-infective agent. It can also help in creating a scab that stops bleeding from a minor skin wound.

subject: Biology
q: Identify which of the following drugs is used to stimulate the heart?
skill: Define pharmacology and drugs, and identify the various sources of drugs
feedback: The correct response is digitalis. Digitalis is a drug that is used to treat heart related conditions such as congestive heart failure (CHF) and arrhythmias. It results in the dilation of blood vessels increasing blood flow throughout the body.

subject: Biology
q: Antibiotics are used for the _____.
options: treatment of viral and bacterial infections
skill: Define pharmacology and drugs, and identify the various sources of drugs
feedback: Antibiotics inhibit or kill bacteria within or on the body and treat bacterial infections e.g. tetracycline, cephalosporin etc.

subject: Biology
q: In a sterile surgery, _____ acid is used to sterilise surgical instruments.
skill: Define pharmacology and drugs, and identify the various sources of drugs
feedback:
Lister successfully introduced carbolic acid (now known as phenol) to sterilise surgical instruments and to clean wounds.

Applying Louis Pasteur's advances in microbiology, Lister championed the use of carbolic acid as an antiseptic, so that it became the first widely used antiseptic in surgery.

subject: Biology
q: The substances used for the treatment, prevention, cure, or diagnosis of a disease are called _____.
skill: Define pharmacology and drugs, and identify the various sources of drugs
feedback: A pharmaceutical drug or medicinal drug is defined as any chemical substance used in the diagnosis, cure, treatment, or prevention of a disease.

subject: Biology
q: _____ is the study of drug composition, properties and medical application.
skill: Define pharmacology and drugs, and identify the various sources of drugs
feedback: Pharmacology is the branch of biology concerned with the study of drug action, where a drug can be broadly defined as any man-made, natural, or endogenous (from within the body) molecule which exerts a biochemical or physiological effect on the cell, tissue, organ, or organism.

subject: Biology
q: _____ reduce the possibility of infections on skin.
skill: Define pharmacology and drugs, and identify the various sources of drugs
feedback: Antiseptics antimicrobial substances that are applied to living tissue/skin to reduce the possibility of infection, sepsis, or putrefaction.

subject: Biology
q: _____ is an example of a _____ class of antibiotics.
skill: Describe the principle usages of painkillers, antibiotics, vaccines and sedatives
feedback: Cephalosporins are bactericidalwhich means that they specifically destroy bacteria. They do so by inhibiting the biosynthesis of the cell wall of bacteria.

subject: Biology
q:
Which of the following statements are correct about antibiotics?
I.    They can reduce bacterial growth.
II.   They are used to increase immunity and sedative effect.
III. They kill bacteria.
skill: Describe the principle usages of painkillers, antibiotics, vaccines and sedatives
feedback: Antibiotics are drugs that used to treat bacterial infections. They act bothin within and on the body to either inhibit or kill bacteria. However, they do not increase immunity, nor do they act as a sedative.

subject: Biology
q: The biosynthesis of bacterial cell-walls is inhibited by _____.
skill: Describe the principle usages of painkillers, antibiotics, vaccines and sedatives
feedback: Cephalosporin is an antibiotic that kills bacteria by inhibiting the synthesis of its cell wall.

subject: Biology
q: ____ is characterized as an analgesic.
skill: Describe the principle usages of painkillers, antibiotics, vaccines and sedatives
feedback: Analgesics are drugs that are used to relieve pain. Common examples include aspirin and paracetamol. Cephalosporin, penicillin, and tetracycline are all examples of antibiotics used to treat bacterial infections.

subject: Biology
q: Who discovered the first antibiotic from the fungus plant?
skill: Describe the principle usages of painkillers, antibiotics, vaccines and sedatives
feedback: Penicillin was the first antibiotic discovered by Alexander Fleming from a fungus called Penicillium notatum. This fungus naturally produces penicillin as a defense against bacteria.

subject: Biology
q: What medicines inhibit or kill bacteria within or on the body?
skill: Describe the principle usages of painkillers, antibiotics, vaccines and sedatives
feedback: Antibiotics are drugs that used to treat bacterial infections. They act bothin within and on the body to either inhibit or kill bacteria. Common examples include penicillin, tetracycline, and ciprofloxacin.

subject: Biology
q: Which of the following are true about vaccines?
options: They protect against any future viral and bacterial infections
They treat the existing bacterial infections only.
They treat existing infections and also protect against future infections.
They protect against viral infections only.
skill: Describe the principle usages of painkillers, antibiotics, vaccines and sedatives
feedback: Vaccines help to develop immunity by imitating an infection, but this imitation infection does not cause any illness. Instead, it causes the immune system to develop the same response as it does to a real infection, so the body can recognise and fight the vaccine preventing disease in the future.

subject: Biology
q: Sulphonamides affect bacteria by
options: stopping the synthesis of a new cell wall.
stopping the synthesis of folic acid.
skill: Describe the principle usages of painkillers, antibiotics, vaccines and sedatives
feedback: Sulpha drugs are synthetic antibiotics that contain sulphonamide group. Sulphonamides is a broad-spectrum antibiotic. They inhibit the folic acid synthesis in bacteria. They are used to treat pneumonia and urinary tract infections.
